sentences.text: OK.
Because if you get warm, and the lights can get warm.
I'm-- I'm fully aware of it.
I can stand anything.
I will-- I-- I-- want you to do that.
If I stray off-- if I stray off--
Good morning.
My name is Martin I.
Selling.
S-E-L-L-I-N-G.
Martin, M-A-R-T-I-N.
It stands for Ignac.
I-G-N-A-C.
Yes, it was.
No.
January 1, 1918.
I was born in Lehrberg, which is a little town in Bavaria.
L-E-H-R-B-E-R-G.
Yes, it's near Ludberg, or actually close to Ansbach, which is the nearest town.
The population was about 1,000.
It was practically one street, very long street between the mountain and the river.
South.
South.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Population was 1,000, you said.
Out of the 1000, how many people were Jewish, approximately?
There were two families, my father's and my brother's-- and his brother's.
It was agricultural.
Very ordinary looking.
Nuremberg.
25 Miles.
Southwest.
Yes.
I would rather call it a village.
Yes, very much, of course.
It was on the main street.
There was only one street.
Do you want to take a picture?
It was a one family home, with a farm attached.
So the business, the textile business was on the first floor, and we lived upstairs.
About a dozen, if you consider every nook and cranny.
It had a yard.
No.
Isidor.
Selling, of course.
He was a merchant.
We had a business there.
Textile.
He was tall, fairly stout, and I only can describe him from pictures.
He died when I was three, and I don't really remember him.
He had a heart attack while he was away on the road.
Ida.
Laub.
L-A-U-B.
No, she was a housewife, and after my father died, she had to run the business.
Also a tall person, very tall for that time, and fairly heavy.
Yes, it was-- we had a very good relationship.
But unfortunately, when we were 10, she started to become sick, and ultimately, the roles became reversed, and I had to take care of her.
It was later diagnosed as Parkinson, which was fairly unknown at that time.
With-- till we were 18.
She died in 1936.
A natural death.
Yes.
I had a twin brother.
Leopold.
And later on, it was-- when we came to the States, he called himself Leo.
Similar to me, only darker.
Very good most of the time, but as twins are, sometimes we had different views and different characters.
And we had to compensate for it.
No.
We did everything together.
We had to.
Our mother dressed us alike and kept us together.
There were very few occasions when we were apart.
No, except in stature.
And even so, we looked different.
Here in United States later on, somebody who did not know he was my brother walked up to him and asked me if we were related.
No, they were all dead.
They were.
Do you-- do you recall their names at all?
Yes.
On both sides.
I made a list of the family history, as much as I could from memory.
Unfortunately, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very soon, when the Nazis came, we were not too interested in the background, and we didn't have time watch-- to do some research, even though I was interested.
But when I came to the United States, much later, after my brother died, I wrote the history of the family as far as I remembered it and from other sources.
And that goes quite a while back.
Oh, sure.
From father's side was Leopold and Teresa.
Her first-- her maiden name was [NON-ENGLISH].
From my mother's side, it was Meyer and Nanni Laub.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- actually, our family goes back up to 1800 and before.
I have traced it.
No, my father's side, they all came from the same area.
He was born in Hornberg.
And I think there are only Sellings there.
He came-- he came-- in Germany, he came from Hornberg.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father's great grandfather, whose name was Isaac, lived-- I think was the first one who adopted the name Selling, because it was after 19-- after 1816, when the Jews in Bavaria could adopt name.
How he got his name, I don't know.
It's so English-sounding, nobody will believe me.

On my mother's side, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her mother's maiden name was Kramer, and they got the name 1816, same time, when Jews taken from Northern Bavaria, when those-- when the Jews were allowed to take family names.
So local Catholic priest asked my ancestor to perpetuate his name by adopting it.
And to reconcile with some other branches of the family.
Regular public school, of course and than after the fourth grade, we went to middle school, [NON-ENGLISH], and I was 16.
It was in 1934, when the Nazis were in power, and I had to leave.
I wanted to study further, but I couldn't.
Yes.
I had only ten.
Personally, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very moderate and not very religious, and now I would call myself an atheist, more or less.
But family history was fairly orthodox.
Orthodox was the only Jewish religion in Germany at that time, especially in the small towns.
There was also a reform congregation in Berlin and Frankfurt.
And people were more or less orthodox, but they all had the orthodox education.
No.
We were only two people.
We had to go to Ansbach, there was a synagogue.
There was-- it had about 25 to 40 members there.
That synagogue there was built 1750 or thereabouts.
When Frederick II, the King of Prussia, married his sister to the Marquis of Ansbach, he built a castle for them, a palace.
And it's the same architect built that synagogue.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
Oh, they were all celebrated.
Our business, of course, was closed for Jewish holidays and on Saturday.
And-- but later on, we drifted a little bit away.
Yes.
Actually, the only-- the-- what stands out most is one of the earliest I remember.
There was-- we had a tradition in our family that our father sat at the head of the table, and our mother sat next to him.
And after he died, nobody was allowed to sit there for years.
And about two years later, at the Seder with an uncle, at the Seder, I and my brother were extremely upset that somebody sat in our father's chair.
That was the first time anybody sat there.
Yeah.
Yes, we did.
Very rich [CHUCKLES] and very stuffing.
It was mostly meat, chicken, everything, everything with heavy sauces.
We usually had wine with it.
And the whole family life more or less centered around eating.
No, but usually it was the same.
It was-- there wasn't so much choice.
It was-- we didn't have the stress on variety as we have here.
So more or less the same thing was cooked every week.
Yes, we lighted the candles and-- that-- [INAUDIBLE] but as our mother became sick, social occasions dwindled.
We-- Nothing was there to do it, and so it just fell on the wayside.
Yes.

And that also is maybe typically of the fact that our father was dead, our mother gravitated more to her side of the family, and so relationship with our mother's family was much more intense than with our father's family.
Actually to all of them, I hated them equally, [CHUCKLES] most of them, at least.
Because what was a fact of life was here we were, the poor little orphans, and everybody wanted to have a part in our education.
So we were pulled left and right and everything, and they-- whatever we did should have been done better.
And we were regimented more than our cousins were.
It was at least-- it wasn't a pleasant childhood.
Did you have a bar mitzvah?
Yes.
Not too much.
It was traditional.
And it was kind of stilted, because two sides of the families met, and there was a little friction.
There always is friction.
Primarily where there is inheritance, there is disagreement.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the relatives of my father were-- had part of the business.
And when-- after he died, it was supposed to be split up, there were disagreements.
And they carried through.
Introverted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and mostly lonely.
I didn't have much fun.
It's-- somehow I don't remember funny thing-- fun things.
No, I read a lot, because I was introverted.
Yes.
My ambition was to become a doctor.
But that was quickly put aside after the Nazis came.
No.
I didn't have any friends.
Being a twin, we were so interdependent on each other, that, at least I didn't need many outside friends.
We had our old house.
After our mother died, our uncle, who lived with us and ran our business continued to live with us.
And even wherever we were, whether it was Wurzburg, or whether it was Munich, our home was Lehrberg.
And we always came back.
And we kept the housekeeper after our mother's death, and she rents the house for us.
Yes.
It was general.
There was nothing too overt or nothing.
It was accepted.
Most countries, and Germany in particular, there was always latent anti-Semitism, just like in the United States and every place else.
You just learned to live with it.
And I took it for granted, just the way I took for granted that my father was dead.
It's part of-- it was part of life.
No.
But it-- it was around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That-- my-- we just were different, which-- which was accepted, and no fuss was made about it.
No, not at all.
See, there's one thing which people don't realize.
That [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were Germans and felt like Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All our [INAUDIBLE], and including my father, were in World War I.
The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother had two brothers.
We considered ourselves Germans.
He was very patriotic.
Even after he was wounded in World War I, he volunteered to go back to the front.
It was his Germany.
My uncle, too, he was part of being [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a veteran of the German wars.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On my father's side, he had two brothers, one who died in Romania, and the other one who was wounded and later who had a head shot.
He later developed epilepsy, and sooner or later, he-- he got more and more-- was more and more affected by it and afflicted by it.
And he died from it in 1932.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's-- when we-- when Hitler came to power, it was a shock.
I remember one thing very vividly, going to school, and I was told I had to sit in the back of the class.
That was--
was in 1933, after Hitler got to power.
And during the last year, I was sitting in the back of the class.
The teachers didn't talk to me too much.
Some were indirectly sympathetic, but that was life.
And when I was 16, I was told frankly, you had all your legal education.
That's it.
So I had to become a-- I had to learn a trade for immigration.
I know-- being a businessman is no-- nothing to immigrate with, so I wanted-- also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all those machine shops and all the factories, they were taken over by the Nazis.
So only thing I really could become was a tailor.
And so finally, I was apprenticed to a little tailor in Wurzburg.
And that was my trade.
I started out when I was 16.
And that was in 1936.
Yeah.
Nobody talked to me.
They were afraid to.
We had a business that suddenly came to a standstill.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we just were outcast.
And we never thought ourselves anything different than German.
That really came as a shock.
Maybe I-- if I-- in Poland for instance, there was-- was always quite a bit of anti-Semitism.
And Jews were Jews.
And considered a nationality, Jews.
We were not.
Our nationality was German.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Period.
I've said enough.
[CHUCKLES] That's all I can say about it.
Yes, when I couldn't avoid it.
I lived in Munich, and Hitler came frequently to Munich.
When he crosses the street, everybody had to stand at attention and shout, Heil, Hitler.
I tried to get to a side street like everybody else and tried to get busy someplace else to avoid him.
It wasn't a matter of wanting to see him.
Yes.
I was a tailor in Munich.
And my boss, a Jew, suddenly left overnight and fled to Italy.
So I had no other place to go.
I went back home to Lehrberg-- Lehrberg.
And I was there with my uncle.
And in the morning, the next-- actually, it wasn't the night, it was the morning after, at 6 o'clock, they came and my uncle and I were both arrested and processed and sent to a prison in Ansbach.
My brother was in Nuremberg with relatives, my other brother.
He was working there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when they left, they came to the house the night before, did a lot of damage, ruined it, broke tables, chairs, and everything, also the china.
And-- but he was not arrested.
I don't know why.
Luckily.
[INAUDIBLE].
When they left, after, when they wanted to have a cup of coffee, they had two cups left and one saucer.
And the table legs, two legs were missing, but there was another one there, which didn't come from that table after they were through destroying everything.
In Nuremberg.
They didn't destroy anything.
They didn't touch anything, but they arrested us.
In the morning, at 6 o'clock.
On the 10th of-- of November.
Except they swiped a few things, like a camera.
And they took our car away and drove it off, and it was never returned.
No.
And since we were only Jews, there-- there was no-- nothing could be warned.
It was all very quiet.
They rang the door.
They arrested us.
I was still in bed.
They were a group of SR men in proud uniforms.
No.
They didn't even know where-- where-- they didn't even know where we are going and when and for how long.
All they were told, go and get those Jews.
And bring them here.
They took us to Ansbach, as I said, and some village children too.
They put us all in a hall.
And at about 3 o'clock in the afternoon, some women and children were let go.
They could go home.
Or everybody over 55.
And the rest were put into the local prison.
It's district prison.
In Ansbach.
In Ansbach.
How-- who-- how many people were you with when you were put into prison?
At that time, we were about 15 people.
Men, yeah.
Regular prison.
It was not very modern.
It was an old fashioned prison.
It was clean.
The facilities were not very good.
But that's what it was.
My uncle and I were in a cell together.
And you were with your--
My uncle.
Your uncle.
How long did you remain in the prison?
Two days.
And then about half of them were released and the other half were shipped to Nuremberg, to the prisons there.
